challenges.title: Email Address Finder
challenges.description: Write a function `find_emails(text)` that uses the `re` module to find all email addresses in a text string. An email matches the pattern: one or more word characters/dots/hyphens, followed by @, followed by one or more word characters/dots/hyphens. Return a list of unique email addresses found, in the order they first appear.
challenges.starter_code:
import re

def find_emails(text):
    """Find all unique email addresses in text, in order of first appearance."""
    # Your code here
    pass
challenges.test_code:
assert find_emails('Contact alice@example.com for info.') == ['alice@example.com']
assert find_emails('No emails here!') == []
text = 'Send to bob@test.org and alice@example.com or bob@test.org again.'
result = find_emails(text)
assert result == ['bob@test.org', 'alice@example.com']
assert find_emails('user.name@domain.co.uk') == ['user.name@domain.co.uk']
assert find_emails('a@b.com, c@d.com, e@f.com') == ['a@b.com', 'c@d.com', 'e@f.com']
assert find_emails('test-user@my-domain.com') == ['test-user@my-domain.com']
multi = find_emails('x@y.com and x@y.com and z@w.com')
assert multi == ['x@y.com', 'z@w.com']
hints: Use re.findall() with a pattern like r'[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+'.
To deduplicate while preserving order, iterate through matches and add to a result list only if not already seen (use a set for tracking).

challenges.title: Config File Parser
challenges.description: Write a function `parse_config(text)` that parses a simple config file format. Each line is either: a key=value pair, a comment (starts with #), or a blank line. Return a dict of key-value pairs. Keys and values should be stripped of whitespace. Lines without '=' (other than comments/blanks) should be ignored. Support values with '=' in them (only split on the first '=').
challenges.starter_code:
def parse_config(text):
    """Parse a key=value config file format.
    
    Rules:
        - Lines starting with # are comments (ignored).
        - Blank lines are ignored.
        - key=value pairs are split on the first '='.
        - Keys and values are stripped of whitespace.
        - Lines without '=' (that aren't comments/blank) are ignored.
    """
    # Your code here
    pass
hints: Split the text into lines with text.splitlines(). For each line, strip it and check if it starts with '#' or is empty.
Use str.split('=', 1) to split on only the first '=' sign, giving you at most two parts.
